title: Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation Quiz
description: The napkin-math skill that makes or breaks a system design interview: turning users into QPS, data into storage, traffic into bandwidth, and knowing the latency numbers every engineer should have memorised.

questions.title: The Number to Memorise
questions.description: The single most useful constant for capacity math.
questions.situation: To convert 'per day' into 'per second' quickly, you need the seconds in a day.
options: About 86,400 (and you usually round to ~100,000 for fast math)
questions.explanation: 24 x 3,600 = 86,400 seconds per day. For fast estimation people round it to ~100,000 (10^5), so 'X per day' divided by 100,000 gives a quick average per-second rate. Memorising ~100K seconds/day is the highest-leverage constant in estimation math.
questions.hint: 24 hours times 3,600 seconds. Then round to a power of ten.

questions.title: Daily Requests to Average QPS
questions.description: The most common conversion in a design interview.
questions.situation: Your API serves 1 billion requests per day, spread fairly evenly.
questions.explanation: 1,000,000,000 / 86,400 ~= 11,600/s, or using the ~100,000 sec/day shortcut, 1e9 / 1e5 = 10,000/s. So roughly 10K average QPS. The shortcut gets you within ~15% of the exact number, which is all estimation needs.

questions.title: Average vs Peak
questions.description: Why you never size for the average.
questions.situation: Your average traffic is 10,000 QPS. You are sizing capacity for the system.
options: Provision for exactly 10,000 QPS, the average
Provision for 100x the average to be totally safe
Provision for a peak that is typically 2-3x the average (or more for spiky workloads), so plan for ~20,000-30,000+ QPS with headroom
Peak does not matter if the average is known
questions.explanation: Traffic is bursty: daily peaks commonly run 2-3x the daily average, and event-driven spikes (launches, news, flash sales) go far higher. Sizing for the average guarantees you fall over at peak. A reasonable default is to estimate peak at 2-3x average and add headroom, then revisit for known spike patterns.
questions.hint: Real traffic has peaks. What multiple of the average is a common rule of thumb?

questions.title: Powers of Two as Data Sizes
questions.situation: You need quick data-size intuition for estimation.
options: They are all roughly equal to a thousand
questions.explanation: 2^10 = 1,024 ~ 1 thousand (a KB), 2^20 = 1,048,576 ~ 1 million (a MB), 2^30 ~ 1 billion (a GB), 2^40 ~ 1 trillion (a TB). Memorising that each 10 powers of two is about 3 orders of magnitude lets you convert between bytes and human numbers instantly.
questions.hint: Every 10 powers of two is roughly 3 zeros in base 10.

questions.title: Sizing a User Table
questions.situation: You have 100 million users. Each user profile row is about 1 KB.
questions.explanation: 100,000,000 users x 1 KB = 100,000,000 KB = ~100 GB (since 1 million KB ~ 1 GB). This kind of users x row-size product is the bread and butter of storage estimation; once you have raw size you multiply by replication factor and add indexes/overhead.
questions.hint: 100 million x 1 KB. A million KB is about a GB.

questions.title: Latency Numbers, Ordered
questions.situation: Order these from fastest to slowest: a main-memory (RAM) reference, an SSD random read, a round trip within the same datacenter, a round trip from California to Europe and back.
options: Same-DC round trip < RAM reference < SSD read < CA-to-Europe round trip
RAM reference (~100 ns) < SSD random read (~16-100 us) < same-DC round trip (~0.5 ms) < CA-to-Europe round trip (~150 ms)
SSD read < RAM reference < CA-to-Europe < same-DC
They are all roughly the same once you account for caching
questions.explanation: The canonical hierarchy: RAM reference ~100 ns, SSD random read ~16-100 us (100-1000x slower than RAM), a same-datacenter round trip ~0.5 ms, and a transcontinental round trip ~150 ms (bounded by the speed of light). Each step is roughly an order of magnitude or more. Knowing this ordering lets you reason about where a design will be slow before you build it.
questions.hint: Memory, then local flash, then local network, then the speed of light across an ocean.

questions.title: DAU to System QPS
questions.situation: A social feed has 100 million daily active users, and each user makes about 10 feed requests per day.
questions.explanation: 100M users x 10 requests = 1 billion requests/day. Divided by ~100,000 sec/day, that is ~10,000 QPS average, and you would then size for ~20-30K+ at peak. Reads usually dominate a feed, so you would also split this into read vs write paths.
questions.hint: Users times requests-per-user gives daily total, then divide by ~100K.

questions.title: Egress Bandwidth
questions.situation: Your CDN origin serves 10,000 images per second, and each image averages 200 KB.
questions.explanation: 10,000 images/s x 200 KB = 2,000,000 KB/s = ~2 GB/s (since a million KB ~ a GB). That is ~16 Gbps of egress, which immediately tells you a single origin will not do and you need a CDN and careful egress-cost planning. Bandwidth estimates are rate x object-size.
questions.hint: 10,000 x 200 KB per second. Convert KB to GB.

questions.title: Daily Log Volume
questions.situation: A service handles 1,000 requests/second and writes a ~1 KB log line per request, all day.
questions.explanation: 1,000 req/s x 1 KB = 1 MB/s of logs. Over 86,400 seconds that is ~86,400 MB ~= 86 GB/day. This is why log retention and sampling matter: a modest 1K QPS service generates tens of GB of logs daily, which is real money at cloud storage and ingestion prices.
questions.hint: Per-second log volume first, then multiply by seconds in a day.

questions.title: Cache Hit Ratio and Effective Latency
questions.situation: A cache hit takes 1 ms and serves 90% of reads; a miss takes 100 ms (it goes to the database). What is the approximate average read latency?
options: About 1 ms, because most reads hit
About 11 ms (0.9 x 1 ms + 0.1 x 100 ms)
questions.explanation: Effective latency is a weighted average: 0.9 x 1 ms + 0.1 x 100 ms = 0.9 + 10 = ~11 ms. Notice the 10% of slow misses dominate the average, that is why raising a 90% hit rate to 99% (0.99 x 1 + 0.01 x 100 = ~2 ms) is such a big win. The tail of misses, not the common hit, drives the number.
questions.hint: Weight each path by its probability and add. Which term dominates?

questions.title: Reading 1 MB Sequentially
questions.situation: Roughly how long to read 1 MB sequentially from main memory versus from a fast SSD?
options: Memory is slower than SSD for sequential reads
Memory: tens of microseconds; SSD: a few hundred microseconds to ~1 ms; both far faster than a network fetch from another continent
questions.explanation: Sequential bandwidth is high: reading 1 MB from RAM is on the order of tens of microseconds, and from a fast SSD on the order of a few hundred microseconds to about a millisecond. Both crush a cross-continent network round trip (~150 ms). The lesson: for bulk data, sequential local reads are cheap; the expensive thing is round trips and random access, not raw bytes.
questions.hint: Sequential local reads are fast. Compare microseconds/ms to a 150 ms ocean round trip.

questions.title: Annual Write Storage
questions.description: Scale a daily rate to a year.
questions.situation: Users post 10 million 300-byte messages per day. Roughly how much raw message storage per year (ignore replication and indexes)?
questions.explanation: 10M x 300 bytes = 3,000,000,000 bytes = ~3 GB/day. Over 365 days that is ~1,000 GB ~= ~1 TB/year of raw message text. You would then multiply by replication factor (often 3x) and add indexing/metadata overhead, but the raw figure is ~1 TB/year.
questions.hint: Daily bytes first (10M x 300), then x365.

questions.title: Round Trips in a Latency Budget
questions.situation: Your p99 latency budget is 200 ms, and each cross-continent round trip costs ~150 ms.
options: You can afford at most one such round trip; a chatty design with multiple sequential cross-continent calls blows the budget, so co-locate or parallelise
You can comfortably fit several cross-continent round trips in the budget
Latency budgets do not apply to distributed calls
Adding more round trips reduces total latency
questions.explanation: With a 200 ms budget and ~150 ms per transcontinental round trip, one round trip already eats most of it; two sequential ones (300 ms) miss entirely. This is why estimation matters before design: you either reduce round trips (batch, co-locate data near compute) or parallelise independent calls so they overlap. Counting round trips against the budget is a core back-of-envelope check.
questions.hint: Divide the budget by the cost of one round trip. How many fit?
